title-group.article-title: Adaptive Control of Grid-Integrated Multi-Source DFIG-Based Microgrids Using Variable Step-Size Quasi-Newton Adaptive Filtering

abstract.p: A multisource microgrid that comprises two doubly fed induction generators, two solar photovoltaic arrays, and battery energy storage is linked to the grid using an advanced variable step size quasi-Newton (VSS Q-Newton) adaptive filter-based grid side control. At the point of common coupling, the controller systematically manages harmonics, regulates power exchange, and maintains power quality (PQ). The VSS Q-Newton method demonstrates robust convergence, improved resilience to PQ disturbances, and efficient harmonic suppression in comparison to existing adaptive filtering techniques such as conventional Q-Newton, Logarithmic Hyperbolic Cosine Adaptive Filter, Least Mean Square, Least Mean Mixed Norm, and d-q-based control. The proposed controller demonstrates a total harmonic distortion of 1.87 %, significantly surpassing traditional control methods. The findings indicate that the VSS Q-Newton method presents a robust solution for real-time management in grid-integrated microgrids incorporating diverse renewable energy sources.

sec.label: I.
sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The growing need for sustainable energy initiatives has drastically accelerated the integration of solar and wind energy in the grid to an unprecedented level. Microgrids (MGs) that combine multiple solar photovoltaic (SPV) arrays, doubly fed induction generator (DFIG)-based wind energy conversion systems (WECS), and battery energy storage (BES) are instrumental in generating sustainable power to the grid [1]. However, integrating these diverse distributed resources into the utility grid introduces challenges, particularly concerning control under varying conditions, overall system stability, and power quality (PQ) [2].

Effective control primarily involves monitoring system performance, anticipating load demands, regulating voltage and frequency, and precisely managing both active and reactive power flow [3]. Innovative control strategies that tackle the inherent variability of renewable energy sources and the harmonic distortions caused by nonlinear loads are essential for ensuring seamless grid integration and superior PQ [4]. Contemporary adaptive filtering in MG control presents a compelling approach to address these challenges [5]. Selfadjusting adaptive filters are particularly advantageous for dynamic MGs because they can automatically adjust their coefficients in response to changing operational environments.

It is important to note that different adaptive algorithms present distinct trade-offs in terms of convergence speed, computational complexity, and steady-state accuracy [6]. Recent developments in adaptive filtering for renewable integration have led to significant enhancements in control performance and PQ. In a grid-connected SPV system, Markala et al. have used a special type of adaptive filter, which is known as the variable step-size logarithmic hyperbolic cosine adaptive filter (VSS-LHCAF), to improve PQ by reducing harmonics, compensating for reactive power, balancing loads, and keeping the grid currents smooth and at a unity power factor. In comparison to traditional least mean square (LMS) and least mean fourth (LMF) algorithms, the proposed method has demonstrated superior convergence characteristics and resulted in reduced total harmonic distortion (THD) in the grid currents [7]. Anbulakshmi et al. implemented a method utilising an artificial neural network (ANN) for maximum power point tracking (MPPT) in conjunction with a least mean mixed norm (LMMN) adaptive controller within a grid-tied wind-PV hybrid system, which has attained low mean squared error and variability [8]. To improve overall efficiency, it has been claimed that connecting DFIG-based wind turbines with PV arrays using a shared DC bus can help lower the number of parts needed and reduce losses. The advantages of Newton-based optimisation for adaptive filters are evident. This method is well-regarded in control systems due to its enhanced convergence speed compared to gradient descent. Yue et al. developed a Newtonian adaptive filtering method utilising a minimal correntropy criterion (MCC), demonstrating quicker convergence and good resilience to impulsive shocks as compared to conventional MCC-based filters [9]. Their investigation confirmed that second-order (Newtonian) methods could significantly enhance the performance of adaptive filters. Furthermore, they conducted an exploration of quasi-Newton methods to overcome the limitations of conventional adaptive algorithms in microgrid applications. Bayat et al. have successfully kept the grid's current THD under 5% when the system is subjected to nonlinear loads by implementing a quasi-Newton control strategy that integrates dynamic step size adjustments and concepts from second-order optimisation. This approach led to a decrease in steady-state error and enhanced convergence [10]. This study uses a variable step size quasi-Newton (VSS Q-Newton) adaptive filtering system to manage the grid side of a multi-source microgrid, which includes two DFGIs, two SPV arrays, a BES with two-way charging, and non-linear loads. The proposed control enhances convergence rate, stability, and PQ through improved real-time energy management and grid synchronisation across various operational contexts.

sec.label: II.
sec.title: System Configuration and Control Algorithms
sec.p: Fig. 1 depicts the arrangement of the microgrid. Two DFIGs are linked to a rotor side converter (RSC), which subsequently interfaces with the microgrid on the DC bus located between the bi-directional converters. The RSC serves two primary functions: it converts the variable-frequency power from the rotor side into DC and oversees the transfer of active and reactive power from the DFIG using the field-orientated control (FOC). Within that DC link, there are two SPV arrays along with a BES that features bidirectional charging capabilities. The grid-side converter (GSC) integrates MG into the grid by using the VSS Q-Newton adaptive filter. A non-linear load is connected at the PCC. The GSC regulates the injection of power into the grid and mitigates issues related to PQ.

sec.label: A.
sec.title: System Overview
sec.p: The grid integrated microgrid setup, designed for lowvoltage applications, incorporates typical household loads alongside its distributed generation capabilities. Each DFIG unit, representative of standard small-scale wind turbines, is rated at 7.5 kW and features 2 pole pairs, operating at a synchronous speed of $157.07 \text{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ (1500 RPM). The BES provides a nominal voltage of 240 V and a capacity of 20 Ah, enabling peak current reduction and bidirectional energy transfer. The SPV array delivers approximately 14.4 kW under standard test conditions $\left(25 C, 1000 W / m^{2}\right)$. A comprehensive compilation of all relevant system specifications, including microgrid features and converter components, is provided in the Appendix. These precise design parameters ensure the DC link voltage is maintained at approximately 400 V, facilitating effective grid integration through the GSC.

sec.label: B.
sec.title: Rotor Side Control of DFIG
sec.p: The $\omega_{r}$ represents the speed of the DFIG, while $\omega_{s}$ denotes the synchronous speed. The $p$ indicates the number of pole pairs, while frequency is represented by $f$. The $\sigma$ represents the DFIG leakage reactance and is indicated by $\sigma=1-\frac{l_{m}^{2}}{l_{s} l_{r}}$

sec.label: C.
sec.title: MPPT of SPV array and Bidirectional charging of BES
sec.p:
The BES and SPV array are interfaced at the DC bus, which lies between the grid-side and rotor-side converters in the proposed microgrid. Fig. 3 illustrates the block diagram governing DC bus voltage ($V_{D C}$) regulation. A single-stage maximum power point tracking (MPPT) approach is employed for the SPV array using the incremental conductance (InC) method [13]. The battery operates bidirectionally, enabling both charging and discharging, while also stabilizing the DC link voltage. While the battery management system (BMS) monitors DC link voltage levels, power flow coordination among the SPV array, BES, and the grid is managed by the grid-side converter, which is discussed in the next section. The battery management system monitors the voltage levels of the DC link. However, the management of power transmission between the SPV array, BES, and grid is handled by the gridside converter, which will be elaborated on in the following section.
Fig. 2.
Block representation of control of rsc

sec.label: D.
sec.title: Control of Grid Side Converter using VSS Q-Newton

sec.label: E.
sec.title: Control of Grid Side Converter (GSC) using VSS Q-Newton

sec.label: III.
sec.title: Simulated Results
sec.p:
A model of a multisource grid interactive $\text{AC} / \text{DC} \text{mi}$-crogrid has been created and simulated utilising the MATLAB/Simulink R2020a environment. This section outlines the results from the simulation, highlighting the dynamic performance of a microgrid under different conditions. In Fig. 5 The initial result presents the VDC, succeeded by the parameters of the grid, non-linear load, battery, SPV array, and DFIG based WECS respectively. At time $\mathrm{t}=1.4 ~\mathrm{s}$, the irradiation is increased from $800 ~\mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$ to $1000 ~\mathrm{W} / \mathrm{m}^{2}$, leading to an increase in the grid side converter current (ivgsc) and the igabc. At $\mathrm{t}=1.6 ~\mathrm{s}$, the wind speed increases from $9 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ to $10 ~\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$, leading to a change in DFIG speed from 141.3rad/s to 162.4rad/s. With a synchronous speed of 1500 RPM (157.07) rad/s, the DFIG shifts its operating region from below synchronous speed operation to above synchronous speed operation. It is also observed from the reversal of rotor current of DFIGa and DFIGb. At time $\mathrm{t}=2.1 ~\mathrm{s}$, the battery transitions from charging to discharging. The increase of current ivgsc and igabc is also evident. Between 2.4 and 2.6 seconds, an unbalancing of the load takes place, leading to a decrease in the equivalent weight of the adaptive filter wnla and wnlabc. Fig. 6 shows the power flow and comparative analysis of VSS Q-Newton with different grid-side control algorithms. In power flow it is observed that with the change in irradiance, wind speed and change in operation of battery (charging-discharging mode), the grid power also changes. The comparison of various control algorithms with VSS Q-Newton shows that VSS Q-Newton consistently reaches a solution faster during the changes caused by an unbalanced load. The FFT analysis shows the total harmonic distortion (THD) of inlabc, and then it shows the THD of the grid current using VSS Q-Newton, traditional Q-Newton, LHCAF, LMMN, LMS, and d-q control methods, in that order. The VSS Q-Newton algorithm demonstrates superior performance relative to traditional Q-Newton, LHCAF, LMMN, LMS, and d-q-based control methods. The d-q based control shows 3.15 % THD of grid current. With a THD of 2.94 %, LMS shows some improvement compared to dq-based control. Attaining a total harmonic distortion of 2.56 %, LMMN shows improved performance relative to LMS. With a THD of 2.38 %, LHCAF shows improved performance compared to LMMN. Performance of the conventional Q-Newton-based control exceeds that of LHCAF, reaching the THD of 2.27 %. The VSS Q-Newton performs better than Q-Newton, LHCAF, LMMN, LMS, and $d q$ based control by achieving a THD of 1.87 %. To further validate the proposed VSS Q-Newton algorithm, its performance was quantitatively compared against conventional Q-Newton, LHCAF, LMMN, LMS, and d-q based control methods. The comparison focused on the THD in the grid current and the estimated convergence time following load disturbances.
Fig. 5.
Simulated result of microgrid with subjected to various transitions
Fig. 6.
Power flow and comparative analysis of vss q-newton based gsc

The THD values were obtained from FFT analysis of the grid current under non-linear loading conditions. For convergence time, the recovery period after the unbalancing of load $t=2.6 ~\mathrm{s}$, as shown in Fig. 6 is analysed in Table 1.

Table I summarizes the comparative results for THD and convergence time respectively.
Table I
Comparative analysis of algorithms: grid current thd and convergence time
Algorithm Grid Current THD (%) Convergence Time (s)
VSS Q-Newton 1.87 0.21
Traditional Q-Newton 2.27 0.27
LHCAF 2.38 0.30
LMMN 2.56 0.33
LMS 2.94 $>0.40$
d-q control 3.15 Not Converged

As presented in Table I, the VSS Q-Newton algorithm consistently demonstrates the lowest THD and significantly faster convergence compared to the other tested methods. This quantitative and qualitative comparison highlights the superior performance of the proposed approach in maintaining power quality and ensuring dynamic stability in grid-integrated microgrids.

sec.label: IV.
sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Integration of multiple renewable sources has been shown into grid with advanced control techniques to address problems with PQ, stability, and adaptive synchronisation. This work shows that in the management of grid-integrated systems including numerous DFIGs, SPV arrays, and BES, the VSS Q-Newton adaptive filtering method has outperformed conventional Q-Newton, LHCAF, LMS, LMMN, and dq-based grid side control. All while maintaining grid current THD at 1.87 % under non-linear loading, the suggested method dynamically adjusts step sizes and low steady-state error. A comparative analysis with alternative grid control techniques has demonstrated the superiority of the VSS Q-Newton based algorithm. The algorithm effectively integrates multiple DFIGs and SPV arrays, ensuring UPF operation and modular scalability, thereby addressing significant gaps in the control of multi-source MGs. Future investigations should delve into its implementation in more extensive hybrid systems and its integration with AI-driven predictive maintenance frameworks to further bolster resilience in smart grid infrastructures.

app.title: Appendix
app.p:
The simulation parameters for the microgrid system are detailed below:
•
PV Array: Series strings: 15; Parallel strings: 6; MPPT Boost Converter Inductance: 3 mH; PV Source Capacitance: $40 \mu ~\mathrm{F}$; Short Circuit Current (per cell): 8.23 A; Open Circuit Voltage (per cell): 32.9 V; Power at MPPT: 14.44 kW. •
Battery Energy Storage (BES): Nominal Voltage: 240 V; Battery Capacity: 20 Ah. •
Doubly Fed Induction Gsenerator (DFIG): Rated Power: 7.5 kW; Number of Poles: 4; Pitch Angle $(\beta): 0^{\circ}$; Synchronous Speed: $157.07 \text{rad} / \mathrm{s}$; Leakage Inductance $\left(l_{s}\right): 0.09 \text{mH}$; Magnetizing Inductance $\left(l_{m}\right): 2.5 \text{mH}$; Stator Resistance $\left(R_{s}\right): 0.282 ~\mathrm{m} \Omega$. •
Grid and DC Bus: Grid Voltage (RMS): 415 V; Grid Frequency: 50 Hz; DC Bus Capacitance $\left(C_{d c}\right): 5400 \mu ~\mathrm{F}$.

ref.mixed-citation: S. Das, B. Singh, and S. Chakraborty, “Seamless multimode control and operation of a dfig-spva-bes-dg based hybrid power conversion system with multiple transition states,” IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics, 2025.

S. K. Pandey and S. Kumar, “Grid-integrated doubly fed wind energy conversion system with photovoltaic array using logarithmic hyperbolic adaptive control,” Computers and Electrical Engineering, vol. 123, no. D, p. 110269, 2025.
D. W. Gao, ed., Basic Concepts and Control Architecture of Microgrids. Energy Storage for Sustainable Microgrid, Academic Press,2015.

V. Gali, N. Gupta, M. J. Ahmadi, M. S. Morey, A. Kural, and P. K. Jamwal, “Experimental investigation of adaptive multi-generalized integrator-based controller for electronically interfaced hybrid microgrid system,” Renewable and Sustainable Energy Reviews, vol. 207, p. 114965, 2025.
L. Tan and J. Jiang, Adaptive Filters and Applications. Digital Signal Processing (Third Edition), Academic Press,2019.

Y.-L. Lee, C.-H. Lin, C.-H. Chang, H.-D. Liu, and C.-C. Chen, “A novel hybrid energy storage system with an adaptive digital filter-based energy management strategy for electric vehicles,” IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification, vol. 10, pp. 5131–5142, Sept. 2024.

M. Karthik, V. R. N. N, and A. K. Panda, “A variable step size logarithmic hyperbolic cosine adaptive filtering-based control scheme for grid-tied pv-dstatcom to enhance power quality,” in 2022 IEEE International Conference on Power Electronics, Drives and Energy Systems (PEDES), (Jaipur, India), pp. 1–6, 2022.

V. Anbulakshmi, G. S. Priya, and K. Punitha, “Lmmn based adaptive control method and ann based mppt method for grid tied wind-pv system,” International Journal of Advanced Research in Electrical, Electronics and Instrumentation Engineering, vol. 8, pp. 2401–2413, Nov. 2019.
P. Yue, H. Qu, J. Zhao, and M. Wang, “Newtonian-type adaptive filtering based on the maximum correntropy criterion,” Entropy, vol. 22, p. 922, Aug. 2020.

M. Bayat, M. M. Koushki, A. A. Ghadimi, M. Tostado-Véliz, and F. Jurado, “Comprehensive enhanced newton raphson approach for power flow analysis in droop-controlled islanded ac microgrids,” Int. J. Electr. Power Energy Syst., vol. 143, p. 108493, Aug. 2022.

S. K. Pandey, K. Verma, S. Kumar, and P. Agnihotri, “Comparative analysis of anfis and pi based control of dfig for wind power generation with grid-integration,” in 2023 9th IEEE India International Conference on Power Electronics (IICPE), pp. 1–7, 2023.
G. Abad, J. López, M. Rodríguez, L. Marroyo, and G. Iwanski, Doubly fed induction machine: modelling and control for wind energy generation. John Wiley & Sons, 2011.

M. A. Shamshuddin, T. S. Babu, T. Dragicevic, M. Miyatake, and N. Rajasekar, “Priority-based energy management technique for integration of solar pv, battery, and fuel cell systems in an autonomous dc microgrid,” Electric Power Components and Systems, vol. 45, pp. 1881–1891, Jan. 2018.
M. Bekrani and H. Zayyani, “A variable step-size regularization-based quasi-newton adaptive filter for system identification,” IEEE Access, vol. 12, pp. 10059–10066, 2024.
